children.title: Understanding field references
children.summary: The log tables are queried with a subset of BigQuery SQL syntax. They all have three columns: `event_message`, `timestamp`, and `metadata`.

children.title: Expanding results
children.summary: Logs returned by queries may be difficult to read in table format. A row can be double-clicked to expand the results into more readable JSON:

children.title: Filtering with [regular expressions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Regular_expression)
children.summary: The Logs use BigQuery Style regular expressions with the [regexp\_contains function](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/reference/standard-sql/string_functi

children.title: Find messages that start with a phrase
children.summary: `^` only looks for values at the start of a string

children.title: Find messages that end with a phrase:
children.summary: `$` only looks for values at the end of the string

children.title: Ignore case sensitivity:
children.summary: `(?i)` ignores capitalization for all proceeding characters

children.title: Wildcards:
children.summary: `.` can represent any string of characters

children.title: Alphanumeric ranges:
children.summary: `[1-9a-zA-Z]` finds any strings with only numbers and letters

children.title: Repeated values:
children.summary: Documentation about Repeated values:

children.title: Escaping reserved characters:
children.summary: `\.` interpreted as period `.` instead of as a wildcard

children.title: `or` statements:
children.summary: `x|y` any string with `x` or `y` present

children.title: `and`/`or`/`not` statements in SQL:
children.summary: `and`, `or`, and `not` are all native terms in SQL and can be used in conjunction with regular expressions to filter results

children.title: Filtering and unnesting example
children.summary: Documentation about Filtering and unnesting example

children.title: Limitations
children.summary: Limitations

children.title: Log tables cannot be joined together
children.summary: Each product table operates independently without the ability to join with other log tables. This may change in the future.

children.title: The `with` keyword and subqueries are not supported
children.summary: The parser does not yet support `with` and subquery statements.

children.title: The `ilike` and `similar to` keywords are not supported
children.summary: Although `like` and other comparison operators can be used, `ilike` and `similar to` are incompatible with BigQuery's variant of SQL. `regexp_contains` can be u

children.title: The wildcard operator `*` to select columns is not supported
children.summary: The log parser is not able to parse the `*` operator for column selection. Instead, you can access all fields from the `metadata` column:

children.summary: Logs are sent as a POST request with a JSON body. Both HTTP/1 and HTTP/2 protocols are supported.

children.title: Allow users to send Presence messages on a channel
children.summary: To update the Presence status for a user create a policy for `insert` on `realtime.messages` where the value of `realtime.messages.extension` is `presence`.

children.title: Protocol messages
children.summary: Messages can be serialized in different formats. The Realtime protocol supports two versions: `1.0.0` and `2.0.0`.
Version 1.0.0 is extremely simple. It uses JSON as the serialization format for messages. The underlying WebSocket messages are all text frames.
Version 2.0.0 uses text and binary WebSocket frames.

children.title: Text frames
children.summary: Text frames are always JSON encoded, but unlike version 1.0.0, they use a JSON array where the element order must be exactly:

children.title: Binary frames
children.summary: The two special message types have a well defined binary format where the first byte defines the type of message. Both are used to send and receive broadcast ev

children.title: User Broadcast
children.summary: Documentation about User Broadcast

children.title: Event types
children.summary: Messages for all events are encoded as text frames using JSON except with the `broadcast` event type which can happen on both text and binary frames.

children.title: Client sent events
children.summary: | Event Type     | Description                                              | Requires Ref | Requires Join Ref |

children.title: phx\_join
children.summary: This is the initial message required to join a channel. The client sends this message to the server to join a specific topic and configure the features it wants

children.title: phx\_leave
children.summary: This message is sent by the client to leave a channel. It can be used to clean up resources or stop listening for events on that channel. Payload should be empt

children.title: heartbeat
children.summary: The heartbeat message should be sent at least every 25 seconds to avoid a connection timeout. Payload should be an empty object.

children.title: access\_token
children.summary: Used to setup a new token to be used by Realtime for authentication and to refresh the token to prevent a private channel from closing when the token expires.

children.title: broadcast (text frame)
children.summary: Used to send a broadcast event to all clients in a channel.

children.title: broadcast (binary frame)
children.summary: See the [User Broadcast Push](#user-broadcast-push) section for the binary frame structure.

children.title: presence
children.summary: Used to send presence metadata after joining a channel. The payload contains the presence information to be tracked by the server.

children.title: Server sent events
children.summary: | Event Type         | Description                                                             | Requires Ref | Requires Join Ref |

children.title: phx\_close
children.summary: This message is sent by the server to signal that the channel has been closed. Payload will be empty object.

children.title: phx\_error
children.summary: This message is sent by the server when an unexpected error occurs in the channel. Payload will be an empty object

children.title: phx\_reply
children.summary: The server sends these messages in response to client requests that require acknowledgment.

children.title: system
children.summary: The server sends system messages to inform clients about the status of their Realtime channel subscriptions.

children.title: broadcast (text frame)
children.summary: {/* supa-mdx-lint-disable-next-line Rule003Spelling */}

children.title: broadcast (binary frame)
children.summary: See the [User Broadcast](#user-broadcast) section for the binary frame structure.

children.title: postgres\_changes
children.summary: The server sends this message when a database change occurs in a subscribed schema and table. The payload contains the details of the change, including the sche

children.title: presence\_state
children.summary: After joining, the server sends a `presence_state` message to a client with presence information. The payload field contains keys, where each key represents a c

children.title: presence\_diff
children.summary: After a change to the presence state, such as a client joining or leaving, the server sends a presence\_diff message to update the client's view of the presence

children.title: `pgmq_public.pop(queue_name)`

children.title: What payment methods are available?

children.summary: All our invoices are issued in USD, but you can pay in any currency so long as the credit card provider allows charging in USD after conversion.

children.title: Can I change the payment method?
children.summary: Yes, you will have to add the new payment method before being allowed to remove the old one.

children.title: Why am I overdue?
children.summary: We were unable to charge your payment method. This likely means that the payment was not successfully processed with the credit card on your account profile.

children.title: Can I delay my payment?
children.summary: No, you cannot delay your payment.

children.title: Can I get a refund of my unused credits?
children.summary: No, we do not provide refunds. Please refer to our [Terms of Service](/terms#1-fees).

children.title: Available authentication email templates
children.summary: There are several authentication-related email templates which can be configured. Each template serves a specific authentication flow:

children.title: `auth.email.template.invite`
children.summary: **Default subject**: "You have been invited"

children.title: `auth.email.template.confirmation`
children.summary: **Default subject**: "Confirm Your Signup"

children.title: `auth.email.template.recovery`
children.summary: **Default subject**: "Reset Your Password"

children.title: `auth.email.template.magic_link`
children.summary: **Default subject**: "Your Magic Link"

children.title: `auth.email.template.email_change`
children.summary: **Default subject**: "Confirm Email Change"

children.title: `auth.email.template.reauthentication`
children.summary: **Default subject**: "Confirm Reauthentication"

children.title: Available security notification email templates
children.summary: There are several security notification email templates which can be configured. These emails are only sent to users if the respective security notifications ha

children.title: `auth.email.notification.password_changed`
children.summary: **Default subject**: "Your password has been changed"

children.title: `auth.email.notification.email_changed`
children.summary: **Default subject**: "Your email address has been changed"

children.title: `auth.email.notification.phone_changed`
children.summary: **Default subject**: "Your phone number has been changed"

children.title: `auth.email.notification.mfa_factor_enrolled`
children.summary: **Default subject**: "A new MFA factor has been enrolled"

children.title: `auth.email.notification.mfa_factor_unenrolled`
children.summary: **Default subject**: "An MFA factor has been unenrolled"

children.title: `auth.email.notification.identity_linked`
children.summary: **Default subject**: "A new identity has been linked"

children.title: `auth.email.notification.identity_unlinked`
children.summary: **Default subject**: "An identity has been unlinked"

children.title: Template variables
children.summary: The templating system provides the following variables for use:

children.title: `ConfirmationURL`
children.summary: Contains the confirmation URL. For example, a signup confirmation URL would look like:

children.title: `Token`
children.summary: Contains a 6-digit One-Time-Password (OTP) that can be used instead of the `ConfirmationURL`.

children.title: `TokenHash`
children.summary: Contains a hashed version of the `Token`. This is useful for constructing your own email link in the email template.

children.title: `SiteURL`
children.summary: Contains your application's Site URL. This can be configured in your project's [authentication settings](/dashboard/project/_/auth/url-configuration).

children.title: `Email`
children.summary: Contains the user's email address.

children.title: `NewEmail`
children.summary: Contains the new user's email address. This is only available in the `email_change` email template.

children.title: `OldEmail`
children.summary: Contains the user's old email address. This is only available in the `email_changed_notification` email template.

children.title: `Phone`
children.summary: Contains the user's new phone number. This is only available in the `phone_changed_notification` email template.

children.title: `OldPhone`
children.summary: Contains the user's old phone number. This is only available in the `phone_changed_notification` email template.

children.title: `Provider`
children.summary: Contains the provider of the newly linked/unlinked identity. This is only available in the `identity_linked_notification` and `identity_unlinked_notification` e

children.title: `FactorType`
children.summary: Contains the type of the newly enrolled/unenrolled MFA factor. This is only available in the `mfa_factor_enrolled_notification` and `mfa_factor_unenrolled_notif

children.title: Architecture
children.summary: Supabase is open source. We choose open source tools which are scalable and make them simple to use.

children.title: Choose your comfort level
children.summary: Our goal at Supabase is to make *all* of Postgres easy to use. That doesn’t mean you have to use all of it. If you’re a Postgres veteran, you’ll probably love t

children.title: Architecture
children.summary: Each Supabase project consists of several tools:

children.title: Postgres (database)
children.summary: Postgres is the core of Supabase. We do not abstract the Postgres database—you can access it and use it with full privileges. We provide tools which make Postgr

children.title: Studio (dashboard)
children.summary: An open source Dashboard for managing your database and services.

children.title: GoTrue (Auth)
children.summary: A JWT-based API for managing users and issuing access tokens. This integrates with PostgreSQL's Row Level Security and the API servers.

children.title: PostgREST (API)
children.summary: A standalone web server that turns your Postgres database directly into a RESTful API.

children.title: Realtime (API & multiplayer)
children.summary: A scalable WebSocket engine for managing user Presence, broadcasting messages, and streaming database changes.

children.title: Storage API (large file storage)
children.summary: An S3-compatible object storage service that stores metadata in Postgres.

children.title: Deno (Edge Functions)
children.summary: A modern runtime for JavaScript and TypeScript.

children.title: `postgres-meta` (database management)
children.summary: A RESTful API for managing your Postgres. Fetch tables, add roles, and run queries.

children.title: Supavisor
children.summary: A cloud-native, multi-tenant Postgres connection pooler.

children.title: Kong (API gateway)
children.summary: A cloud-native API gateway, built on top of NGINX.

children.title: Product principles
children.summary: It is our goal to provide an architecture that any large-scale company would design for themselves,

children.title: Everything works in isolation
children.summary: Each system must work as a standalone tool with as few moving parts as possible.

children.title: Everything is integrated
children.summary: Supabase is composable. Even though every product works in isolation, each product on the platform needs to 10x the other products.

children.title: Everything is extensible
children.summary: We're deliberate about adding a new tool, and prefer instead to extend an existing one.

children.title: Everything is portable
children.summary: To avoid lock-in, we make it easy to migrate in and out. Our cloud offering is compatible with our self-hosted product.

children.title: Play the long game
children.summary: We sacrifice short-term wins for long-term gains. For example, it is tempting to run a fork of Postgres with additional functionality which only our customers n

children.title: Build for developers
children.summary: "Developers" are a specific profile of user: they are *builders*.

children.title: Support existing tools
children.summary: Supabase supports existing tools and communities wherever possible. Supabase is more like a "community of communities" - each tool typically has its own communi

children.title: Features
children.summary: This is a non-exhaustive list of features that Supabase provides for every project.

children.title: Database
children.summary: Database

children.title: Postgres database
children.summary: Every project is a full Postgres database. [Docs](/docs/guides/database).

children.title: Vector database
children.summary: Store vector embeddings right next to the rest of your data. [Docs](/docs/guides/ai).

children.title: Auto-generated REST API via PostgREST
children.summary: RESTful APIs are auto-generated from your database, without a single line of code. [Docs](/docs/guides/api#rest-api-overview).

children.title: Auto-generated GraphQL API via pg\_graphql
children.summary: Fast GraphQL APIs using our custom Postgres GraphQL extension. [Docs](/docs/guides/graphql/api).

children.title: Database webhooks
children.summary: Send database changes to any external service using Webhooks. [Docs](/docs/guides/database/webhooks).

children.title: Secrets and encryption
children.summary: Encrypt sensitive data and store secrets using our Postgres extension, Supabase Vault. [Docs](/docs/guides/database/vault).

children.title: Replication
children.summary: Automatically replicate your database to external destinations like data warehouses and analytics platforms. [Docs](/docs/guides/database/replication/replicatio

children.title: Platform
children.summary: Platform

children.title: Database backups
children.summary: Projects are backed up daily with the option to upgrade to Point in Time recovery. [Docs](/docs/guides/platform/backups).

children.title: Custom domains
children.summary: White-label the Supabase APIs to create a branded experience for your users. [Docs](/docs/guides/platform/custom-domains).

children.title: Network restrictions
children.summary: Restrict IP ranges that can connect to your database. [Docs](/docs/guides/platform/network-restrictions).

children.title: SSL enforcement
children.summary: Enforce Postgres clients to connect via SSL. [Docs](/docs/guides/platform/ssl-enforcement).

children.title: Branching
children.summary: Use Supabase Branches to test and preview changes. [Docs](/docs/guides/platform/branching).

children.title: Terraform provider
children.summary: Manage Supabase infrastructure via Terraform, an Infrastructure as Code tool. [Docs](/docs/guides/platform/terraform).

children.title: Read replicas
children.summary: Deploy read-only databases across multiple regions, for lower latency and better resource management. [Docs](/docs/guides/platform/read-replicas).

children.title: Log drains
children.summary: Export Supabase logs to 3rd party providers and external tooling. [Docs](/docs/guides/platform/log-drains).

children.title: Studio
children.summary: Studio

children.title: Studio Single Sign-On
children.summary: Login to the Supabase dashboard via SSO. [Docs](/docs/guides/platform/sso).

children.title: Realtime
children.summary: Realtime

children.title: Postgres changes
children.summary: Receive your database changes through WebSockets. [Docs](/docs/guides/realtime/postgres-changes).

children.title: Broadcast
children.summary: Send messages between connected users through WebSockets. [Docs](/docs/guides/realtime/broadcast).

children.title: Presence
children.summary: Synchronize shared state across your users, including online status and typing indicators. [Docs](/docs/guides/realtime/presence).

children.title: Auth
children.summary: Auth

children.title: Email login
children.summary: Build email logins for your application or website. [Docs](/docs/guides/auth/auth-email).

children.title: Social login
children.summary: Provide social logins - everything from Apple, to GitHub, to Slack. [Docs](/docs/guides/auth/social-login).

children.title: Phone logins
children.summary: Provide phone logins using a third-party SMS provider. [Docs](/docs/guides/auth/phone-login).

children.title: Passwordless login
children.summary: Build passwordless logins via magic links for your application or website. [Docs](/docs/guides/auth/auth-magic-link).

children.title: Authorization via Row Level Security
children.summary: Control the data each user can access with Postgres Policies. [Docs](/docs/guides/database/postgres/row-level-security).

children.title: CAPTCHA protection
children.summary: Add CAPTCHA to your sign-in, sign-up, and password reset forms. [Docs](/docs/guides/auth/auth-captcha).

children.title: Server-Side Auth
children.summary: Helpers for implementing user authentication in popular server-side languages and frameworks like Next.js, SvelteKit and Remix. [Docs](/docs/guides/auth/server-

children.title: Storage
children.summary: Storage

children.title: File storage
children.summary: Supabase Storage makes it simple to store and serve files. [Docs](/docs/guides/storage).

children.title: Content Delivery Network
children.summary: Cache large files using the Supabase CDN. [Docs](/docs/guides/storage/cdn/fundamentals).

children.title: Smart Content Delivery Network
children.summary: Automatically revalidate assets at the edge via the Smart CDN. [Docs](/docs/guides/storage/cdn/smart-cdn).

children.title: Image transformations
children.summary: Transform images on the fly. [Docs](/docs/guides/storage/serving/image-transformations).

children.title: Resumable uploads
children.summary: Upload large files using resumable uploads. [Docs](/docs/guides/storage/uploads/resumable-uploads).

children.title: S3 compatibility
children.summary: Interact with Storage from tool which supports the S3 protocol. [Docs](/docs/guides/storage/s3/compatibility).

children.title: Edge Functions
children.summary: Edge Functions

children.title: Deno Edge Functions
children.summary: Globally distributed TypeScript functions to execute custom business logic. [Docs](/docs/guides/functions).

children.title: Regional invocations
children.summary: Execute an Edge Function in a region close to your database. [Docs](/docs/guides/functions/regional-invocation).

children.title: NPM compatibility
children.summary: Edge functions natively support NPM modules and Node built-in APIs. [Link](/blog/edge-functions-node-npm).

children.title: Project management
children.summary: Project management

children.title: CLI
children.summary: Use our CLI to develop your project locally and deploy to the Supabase Platform. [Docs](/docs/reference/cli).

children.title: Management API
children.summary: Manage your projects programmatically. [Docs](/docs/reference/api).

children.title: Client libraries
children.summary: Official client libraries for [JavaScript](/docs/reference/javascript/start), [Flutter](/docs/reference/dart/initializing) and [Swift](/docs/reference/swift/int

children.title: Feature status
children.summary: Supabase Features are in 4 different states - Private Alpha, Public Alpha, Beta and Generally Available.

children.title: Private alpha
children.summary: Features are initially launched as a private alpha to gather feedback from the community. To join our early access program, send an email to [product-ops@supaba

children.title: Public alpha
children.summary: The alpha stage indicates that the API might change in the future, not that the service isn’t stable. Even though the [uptime Service Level Agreement](/sla) doe

children.title: Beta
children.summary: Features in Beta are tested by an external penetration tester for security issues. The API is guaranteed to be stable and there is a strict communication proces

children.title: Generally available
children.summary: In addition to the Beta requirements, features in GA are covered by the [uptime SLA](/sla).

children.title: Edge Functions Architecture
children.summary: Understanding the Architecture of Supabase Edge Functions

children.title: 1. Understanding Edge Functions through an example: Image filtering
children.summary: To illustrate how edge functions operate, consider a photo-sharing app where users upload images and apply filters (e.g., grayscale or sepia) before saving them

children.title: 2. Deployment process
children.summary: Deploying an edge function is straightforward and automated, requiring no manual server setup.

children.title: 3. Global distribution and routing
children.summary: Edge functions leverage a distributed architecture to minimize latency by running code close to the user.

children.title: 4. Execution mechanics: Fast and isolated
children.summary: The core of edge functions' efficiency lies in their execution environment, which prioritizes speed, isolation, and scalability.

children.title: Ephemeral storage
children.summary: Ephemeral storage will reset on each function invocation. This means the files you write during an invocation can only be read within the same invocation.

children.title: Status codes
children.summary: Understand HTTP status codes returned by Edge Functions to properly debug issues and handle responses.

children.title: Success Responses
children.summary: Success Responses

children.title: 2XX Success
children.summary: Your Edge Function executed successfully and returned a valid response. This includes any status code in the 200-299 range that your function explicitly returns

children.title: 3XX Redirect
children.summary: Your Edge Function used the `Response.redirect()` API to redirect the client to a different URL. This is a normal response when implementing authentication flow

children.title: Client Errors
children.summary: These errors indicate issues with the request itself, which typically require changing how the function is called.

children.title: 401 Unauthorized
children.summary: **Cause:** The Edge Function has JWT verification enabled, but the request was made with an invalid or missing JWT token.

children.title: 404 Not Found
children.summary: **Cause:** The requested Edge Function doesn't exist or the URL path is incorrect.

children.title: 405 Method Not Allowed
children.summary: **Cause:** You're using an unsupported HTTP method. Edge Functions only support: `GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `PATCH`, `DELETE`, and `OPTIONS`.

children.title: Server Errors
children.summary: These errors indicate issues with the function execution or underlying platform.

children.title: 500 Internal Server Error
children.summary: **Cause:** Your Edge Function threw an uncaught exception (`WORKER_ERROR`).

children.title: 503 Service Unavailable
children.summary: **Cause:** Your Edge Function failed to start (`BOOT_ERROR`).

children.title: 504 Gateway Timeout
children.summary: **Cause:** Your Edge Function didn't respond within the [request timeout limit](/docs/guides/functions/limits).

children.title: 546 Resource Limit (Custom Error Code)
children.summary: **Cause:** Your Edge Function execution was stopped due to exceeding resource limits (`WORKER_LIMIT`). Edge Function logs should provide which [resource limit](

children.title: Managing JSON and unstructured data

children.summary: Postgres supports two types of JSON columns: `json` (stored as a string) and `jsonb` (stored as a binary). The recommended type is `jsonb` for almost all cases.

children.title: Before you begin

children.title: Migration overview
children.summary: 1.  Create a new partitioned table.

children.title: Example: Migrate `messages` from hypertable to native partitions
children.summary: This example assumes a `messages` hypertable partitioned by `sent_at`.

children.title: 1. Rename the existing hypertable
children.summary: This keeps the original data in place while you create a new partitioned table with the original name.

children.title: 2. Create a new partitioned table
children.summary: When using native partitioning, the partitioning column must be included in any unique index (including the primary key).

children.title: 3. Copy data into the new table
children.summary: For large tables, consider copying in batches (for example by time range) during a maintenance window.

children.title: 4. Drop the old hypertable (and TimescaleDB)
children.summary: Only drop the extension once you’ve migrated all hypertables and no other objects depend on it.

children.title: 5. Configure `pg_partman` (optional)
children.summary: Enable `pg_partman` and register your table so partitions are created ahead of time.

children.summary: `pg_partman` requires running maintenance to pre-make partitions and apply retention policies.

children.title: Partitioning tables
children.summary: Table partitioning is a technique that allows you to divide a large table into smaller, more manageable parts called “partitions”.

children.title: Benefits of table partitioning
children.summary: **Improved query performance:** allows queries to target specific partitions, reducing the amount of data scanned and improving query execution time.

children.summary: When you query the parent table, Postgres automatically routes the query to the relevant partitions based on the conditions specified in the query. This allows

children.title: Solutions: \[#solution-timed-out-fetching-a-new-connection]
children.summary: **Increase the pool timeout**: Increase the `pool_timeout` parameter in your Prisma configuration to give the pooler more time to allocate connections.

children.title: Solutions: \[#solution-server-has-closed-the-connection]
children.summary: **Limit row return sizes**: Try to limit the total amount of rows returned for particularly large requests.

children.summary: You can specify delete policies with the `using` clause.

children.title: Error Codes

children.title: Auth error codes
children.summary: Supabase Auth can return various errors when using its API. This guide explains how to handle these errors effectively across different programming languages.

children.title: Error types
children.summary: Supabase Auth errors are generally categorized into two main types:

children.title: HTTP status codes
children.summary: Below are the most common HTTP status codes you might encounter, along with their meanings in the context of Supabase Auth:

children.title: [403 Forbidden](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Status/403)
children.summary: Sent out in rare situations where a certain Auth feature is not available for the user, and you as the developer are not checking a precondition whether that AP

children.title: [422 Unprocessable Entity](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Status/422)
children.summary: Sent out when the API request is accepted, but cannot be processed because the user or Auth server is in a state where it cannot satisfy the request.

children.title: [429 Too Many Requests](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Status/429)
children.summary: Sent out when rate-limits are breached for an API. You should handle this status code often, especially in functions that authenticate a user.

children.title: [500 Internal Server Error](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Status/500)
children.summary: Indicate that the Auth server's service is degraded. Most often it points to issues in your database setup such as a misbehaving trigger on a schema, function,

children.title: [501 Not Implemented](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Status/501)
children.summary: Sent out when a feature is not enabled on the Auth server, and you are trying to use an API which requires it.

children.title: Best practices for error handling
children.summary: Always use `error.code` and `error.name` to identify errors, not string matching on error messages.
